claims.claim: The Kelau Dam was built to provide clean water to Klang Valley.
claims.source: JICA ODA project page, 'Pahang-Selangor Raw Water Transfer Project', accessed 2026-05-17, https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html; JKR project PDF, 'Pahang-Selangor Raw Water Transfer Project', https://epsmg.jkr.gov.my/images/7/78/RAW_WATER.pdf
claims.correction: The project was built to transfer raw water from Pahang to Selangor/Kuala Lumpur/Putrajaya, with treatment handled downstream; calling it 'clean water' overstates the immediate function of Kelau Dam itself.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found; this is a scope/wording issue rather than a stale figure.

claims.claim: 340 Orang Asli families resettled for the Kelau Dam project in Pahang have been without reliable clean water since 2019.
claims.source: Nor-Hisham Md Saman and Peter Ho, peer-reviewed article, International Review for Spatial Planning and Sustainable Development 3(1), 2015, DOI:10.14246/irspsd.3.1_98, via https://www.beijingcitylab.com/download/vol3no1IRSPSD.pdf; Journal of Peasant Studies article, 2016, DOI:10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559, https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559
claims.correction: The best-located scholarly sources identify 330 Orang Asli people and 137 heads of family in the Lurah Bilut resettlement, while the Ministry figure cited in the Journal of Peasant Studies was 84 and the Department of Orang Asli Affairs figure was 137 before resettlement. I found no primary source for 340 families or the 'since 2019' clean-water claim.
claims.stale_check: The analysis was derived on 2026-05-17. I found no evidence that a recent update changed the family count to 340.

claims.claim: The displaced Orang Asli families were promised piped supply as part of resettlement.
claims.source: No primary source found. Related official planning source: Prime Minister's Department/Economic Planning Unit, Mid-Term Review of the Eighth Malaysia Plan 2001-2005, https://www.pmo.gov.my/dokumenattached/RMK/RMK8_MTR.pdf
claims.correction: The official planning document confirms an EIA for converting 600 acres of Kelau Forest Reserve at Sungai Bilut for resettlement of Sungai Temir Orang Asli, but I found no primary text promising piped or treated water supply to the resettled families.

claims.claim: Resettlement houses were built without connection to the treated water network.
claims.source: Government response to SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019, published by SUHAKAM, pp. 22-24, https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/maklumbalas-kerajaan2019.pdf
claims.correction: Government sources verify broader Orang Asli treated-water access gaps, including 147 Orang Asli villages without treated water systems as of October 2020, but I found no primary source specifically confirming the Kelau/Lurah Bilut houses lacked treated-water network connections.
claims.stale_check: No recent specific update found for this settlement.

claims.claim: Residents rely on untreated stream water.
claims.source: No primary source found for the Kelau/Lurah Bilut settlement. Broader context: Government response to SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019, https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/maklumbalas-kerajaan2019.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced broader claim, or obtain field/official evidence for this specific community.

claims.claim: Waterborne illness rates are 4x the state average.
claims.source: No primary source found after searching for Kelau, Sungai Temir, Lurah Bilut, Pahang Orang Asli and waterborne illness rate comparisons.
claims.correction: Remove unless a Health Ministry dataset, epidemiological study, or official health report can be cited with the exact numerator, denominator, disease definition, geography and year.

claims.claim: RM82M was allocated for the resettlement programme.
claims.source: JICA ODA project page records a loan agreement amount of 82.040 billion yen for the Pahang-Selangor Raw Water Transfer Project, not RM82 million for resettlement: https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html
claims.correction: I found no primary source for RM82 million allocated to the resettlement programme. The visible official figure resembling this is 82.040 billion yen for the overall water-transfer project loan.

claims.claim: Audit found RM31M spent on road access and administration.
claims.source: No primary audit source found in searches for Kelau/Sungai Temir/Lurah Bilut/RM31M.
claims.correction: Do not publish this figure without the exact audit report title, year, page and auditing body.

claims.claim: Audit found RM8M spent on housing.
claims.source: No primary audit source found in searches for Kelau/Sungai Temir/Lurah Bilut/RM8M.
claims.correction: Do not publish this figure without the exact audit report title, year, page and auditing body.

claims.claim: Audit found RM0 spent on water connection.
claims.source: No primary audit source found in searches for Kelau/Sungai Temir/Lurah Bilut/water connection spending.
claims.correction: Remove unless an official audit table explicitly states zero allocation or expenditure for water connection.

claims.claim: The dam supplies 1.1 billion litres daily to 4 million urban consumers.
claims.source: JKR project PDF states the Semantan intake pumping capacity as 12 units x 3.5 m3/s and Kelau Dam effective storage of 135.5 million m3, https://epsmg.jkr.gov.my/images/7/78/RAW_WATER.pdf; JICA project page says the Selangor/Kuala Lumpur area had about 4.73 million people, https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html; Air Selangor Sustainability Report 2020 mentions Langat 2 expected production capacity of 700 MLD, https://www.airselangor.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/Air-Selangor-Sustainability-Report-2020.pdf
claims.correction: I found official/authoritative figures of 4.73 million people in the target area, 700 MLD expected Langat 2 production capacity, and other project design capacities, but not a primary source for 1.1 billion litres daily to exactly 4 million consumers. State the sourced capacity and population precisely.
claims.stale_check: Capacity and serviced-population figures may change with later phases, but no source found supporting the exact 1.1 billion/4 million wording as of 2026-05-17.

claims.source: The 85,000 litres/day number is mathematically consistent with 340 households x 250 litres/day, but the underlying 340-family count is contradicted by peer-reviewed sources: https://www.beijingcitylab.com/download/vol3no1IRSPSD.pdf; https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559
claims.correction: Because the family count is unsupported, the derived daily-need figure is not reliable. If estimating need, disclose the assumption and use the verified household count or explain why a newer count is authoritative.
claims.stale_check: No recent verified count found changing the settlement to 340 families.

claims.claim: The dam's output is 12,900 times greater than community need.
claims.source: This ratio is derived from 1.1 billion litres/day divided by 85,000 litres/day. The underlying figures were not verified; see JKR project PDF, https://epsmg.jkr.gov.my/images/7/78/RAW_WATER.pdf, and JICA project page, https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html
claims.correction: The ratio should be removed unless both numerator and denominator are sourced. It is a calculated rhetorical comparison built on unverified inputs.

claims.claim: The dam serves 4 million people but the 340 displaced families receive neither clean water nor adequate compensation.
claims.source: JICA project page supports a target area population of about 4.73 million, not exactly 4 million: https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html; Journal of Peasant Studies supports contested resettlement and compensation issues but not this exact claim: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559
claims.correction: Break this into sourced components. The '4 million' figure should be corrected to the sourced population or removed; '340 families', 'neither clean water', and 'adequate compensation' need primary support.

claims.claim: Displacement for infrastructure must meet minimum standards: equivalent or better services, monitored for 10 years.
claims.source: No Malaysian legal or regulatory primary source found establishing this exact 10-year monitoring standard for Kelau-type resettlement. Related planning source: PMO/EPU Mid-Term Review of the Eighth Malaysia Plan, https://www.pmo.gov.my/dokumenattached/RMK/RMK8_MTR.pdf
claims.correction: Frame as a normative recommendation unless citing a specific Malaysian law, EIA condition, lender safeguard, or resettlement policy that requires equivalent-or-better services and 10 years of monitoring.

claims.source: Journal of Peasant Studies, 2016, DOI:10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559, describes land being 'usurped' and institutional failures, but this is an analytical/normative conclusion rather than a legal finding: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559
claims.correction: Label this as opinion or argument. Do not present it as a verified legal conclusion unless supported by a court ruling or official legal finding.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 340 Orang Asli families resettled for the Kelau Dam project in Pahang have been without reliable clean water since 2019
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The exact family count, water status, and 2019 start date are not traceable to a primary source; peer-reviewed sources indicate 330 people and 137 heads of family, not 340 families.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Nor-Hisham Md Saman and Peter Ho, IRSPSD 2015, DOI:10.14246/irspsd.3.1_98, https://www.beijingcitylab.com/download/vol3no1IRSPSD.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The Kelau Dam was built to provide clean water to Klang Valley.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official sources describe a raw-water transfer project, not direct provision of clean/treated water by the dam.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Waterborne illness rates are 4x the state average.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary health dataset or report was found for this exact rate comparison.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: RM82M was allocated for the resettlement programme.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source found for RM82 million resettlement allocation; the nearest official figure is 82.040 billion yen for the overall project loan.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Audit found RM31M spent on road access and administration. RM8M on housing. RM0 on water connection.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No audit report or official expenditure table was found for these figures.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The dam supplies 1.1 billion litres daily to 4 million urban consumers. 340 displaced families need 85,000 litres daily. The dam's output is 12,900 times greater than community need.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The comparison uses unverified or contradicted inputs and converts them into a precise ratio, giving a false impression of audited numerical certainty.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: JKR project PDF, https://epsmg.jkr.gov.my/images/7/78/RAW_WATER.pdf; JICA ODA project page, https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The dam serves 4 million people but the 340 displaced families receive neither clean water nor adequate compensation
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The underlying population and family figures are inaccurate or unsupported, and the clean-water/compensation assertions are not verified from primary sources.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: JICA ODA project page, https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html; Journal of Peasant Studies, DOI:10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559

omitted_facts: Official planning documents state that an EIA study covered the proposed conversion of 600 acres of Kelau Forest Reserve at Sungai Bilut for resettlement of Sungai Temir Orang Asli: PMO/EPU Mid-Term Review of the Eighth Malaysia Plan 2001-2005, https://www.pmo.gov.my/dokumenattached/RMK/RMK8_MTR.pdf

JICA records the Pahang-Selangor Raw Water Transfer Project loan agreement in March 2005 with a loan amount of 82.040 billion yen, which is materially different from RM82 million for resettlement: https://www.jica.go.jp/oda/project/MXIX-1/index.html

Peer-reviewed sources report competing household counts for the Orang Asli resettlement: 84 recorded by the Ministry of Energy, Green Technology and Water and 137 by the Department of Orang Asli Affairs before resettlement; this should have been included before using any '340 families' figure: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/03066150.2016.1253559

A 2019 government response to SUHAKAM states that, as of October 2020, 82.7% or 706 Orang Asli villages had clean and treated water systems, 131 villages had malfunctioning systems needing maintenance, and 17.2% or 147 villages lacked treated-water systems; this provides broader context but not proof for Kelau specifically: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/maklumbalas-kerajaan2019.pdf

Official/technical project figures found include Kelau Dam effective storage of 135.5 million m3 and Semantan intake pumping capacity data, rather than the analysis's 1.1 billion litres/day claim: https://epsmg.jkr.gov.my/images/7/78/RAW_WATER.pdf
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only 'T4A related issue 1971', which is circular and not a primary source. It does not cite government planning documents, JICA loan records, JKR technical material, health data, audit reports, court records, community testimony, JAKOA records, SUHAKAM materials, or peer-reviewed research. Political and institutional perspectives are almost absent: no federal/state water agencies, Orang Asli representatives, independent researchers, auditors, health authorities, or project financiers are directly sourced. Source diversity is therefore extremely weak.
overall_assessment: The analysis has serious factual reliability problems. The broad theme that Kelau involved Orang Asli resettlement for a Pahang-Selangor raw-water project is supported, but most precise claims readers would rely on are unverified, contradicted, or framed too strongly. The most damaging issues are the unsupported '340 families' count, invented-looking audit/expenditure figures, the unsourced waterborne-illness statistic, and a precise output-to-community-need ratio built from unstable inputs. This should not be published in its current factual form without replacing the numerical cards with sourced official or peer-reviewed evidence.
